source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
<image_1>

In certain lakes there is a strange phenomenon called "seiching" which is an oscillation of the water. Lakes in which you can see this phenomenon are normally long compared with the depth and also narrow. It is natural to see waves in a lake but not something like the seiching, where the entire water volume oscillates, like the coffee in a cup that you carry to a waiting guest.

In order to create a model of the seiching we look at water in a rectangular container. The length of the container is $L$ and the depth of the water is $h$. Assume that the surface of the water to begin with makes a small angle with the horizontal. The seiching will then start, and we assume that the water surface continues to be plane but oscillates around an axis in the horizontal plane and located in the middle of the container.

Create a model of the movement of the water and derive a formula for the oscillation period $T$. The starting conditions are given in figure above.

Assume that $\xi<<h$. The table below shows experimental oscillation periods for different water depths in two containers of different lengths. Check in some reasonable way how well the formula that you have derived agrees with the experimental data. Give your opinion on the quality of your model.

Table 1. $L=479 \mathrm{~mm}$

| $h / m m$ | 30 | 50 | 69 | 88 | 107 | 124 | 142 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $T / s$ | 1.78 | 1.40 | 1.18 | 1.08 | 1.00 | 0.91 | 0.82 |

Table 2. $L=143 \mathrm{~mm}$

| $h / m m$ | 31 | 38 | 58 | 67 | 124 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | ---: |
| $T / s$ | 0.52 | 0.52 | 0.43 | 0.35 | 0.28 |

The graph below shows results from measurements in lake Vättern in Sweden. This lake has a length of $123 \mathrm{~km}$ and a mean depth of $50 \mathrm{~m}$. What is the time scale in the graph?

<image_2>

The water surface level in Bastudalen (northern end of lake Vättern) and Jönköping (southern end).
extraction_notes: 答案字段为选项字母 B，已根据 options 顺序映射为对应选项文本。
记录中存在 <image_1>、<image_2> 占位符和 PIL.Image 对象描述，但没有可直接提取的图片路径或 URL。

image_dependency: Question explicitly refers to 'the figure above' for starting conditions and 'The graph below' for the lake data
The task includes interpreting experimental tables and a graph, indicating nontrivial visual/table dependence
asset_registry_record shows an image exists and registry_passed=true
multi_step: The prompt asks to create a model, derive a formula for T, compare it against experimental data, and infer a time scale from a graph
Solving requires combining physical assumptions (small angle, plane surface), derivation, and data/graph interpretation
The presence of two data tables for different container lengths suggests at least one additional validation step beyond formula derivation
verifiability: answer_type=short_text and answer_present=true, so the final answer can be string-matched or formula-checked
choices are provided, which can make answer checking easier if treated as a multiple-choice target
registry_passed=true, so the sample assets are available for downstream checking
However, the question is multi-part while the provided answer is only a single formula, which weakens end-to-end answer completeness verification

decision_reasons: Question, image, and answer assets are all present and registry passed, so the core semantics appear recoverable.
The sample looks valuable because it is strongly image-dependent and multi-step.
However, the question is multi-part while the provided answer is only a short single formula, creating a question-answer scope mismatch.
This weakens end-to-end verifiability, so it should be retained with lower priority rather than rejected.

normalization_record.normalized_question_text:
In certain lakes there is a strange phenomenon called "seiching" which is an oscillation of the water. Lakes in which you can see this phenomenon are normally long compared with the depth and also narrow. It is natural to see waves in a lake but not something like the seiching, where the entire water volume oscillates, like the coffee in a cup that you carry to a waiting guest.

In order to create a model of the seiching we look at water in a rectangular container. The length of the container is $L$ and the depth of the water is $h$. Assume that the surface of the water to begin with makes a small angle with the horizontal. The seiching will then start, and we assume that the water surface continues to be plane but oscillates around an axis in the horizontal plane and located in the middle of the container.

Create a model of the movement of the water and derive a formula for the oscillation period $T$. The starting conditions are given in the figure above.

Assume that $\xi \ll h$. The table below shows experimental oscillation periods for different water depths in two containers of different lengths. Check in some reasonable way how well the formula that you have derived agrees with the experimental data. Give your opinion on the quality of your model.

Table 1. $L = 479\ \mathrm{mm}$

| $h / \mathrm{mm}$ | 30 | 50 | 69 | 88 | 107 | 124 | 142 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $T / \mathrm{s}$ | 1.78 | 1.40 | 1.18 | 1.08 | 1.00 | 0.91 | 0.82 |

Table 2. $L = 143\ \mathrm{mm}$

| $h / \mathrm{mm}$ | 31 | 38 | 58 | 67 | 124 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $T / \mathrm{s}$ | 0.52 | 0.52 | 0.43 | 0.35 | 0.28 |

The graph below shows results from measurements in lake Vättern in Sweden. This lake has a length of $123\ \mathrm{km}$ and a mean depth of $50\ \mathrm{m}$. What is the time scale in the graph?

The water surface level in Bastudalen (northern end of lake Vättern) and Jönköping (southern end).

agent_assessment.rationale: The problem statement and diagram are understandable: the image clearly shows the rectangular container, water depth h, length L, and surface tilt ξ. However, the prompt asks for several things (derive a model and period, compare with data, evaluate the model, and infer a graph time scale), while the provided answer is only a single formula, so the expected final answer is ambiguous.

cleaning_records.decision_rationale: The sample is understandable and recoverable: the figure/text support the seiching model, the isolated rewrite asks for a single formula, and the provided answer aligns well with that rewritten target. However, the original prompt is multipart while the answer covers only one subtask, so the annotation target is not cleanly normalized without the split-open rewrite.

field_audit_records.before_value:
<image_1>

In certain lakes there is a strange phenomenon called "seiching" which is an oscillation of the water. Lakes in which you can see this phenomenon are normally long compared with the depth and also narrow. It is natural to see waves in a lake but not something like the seiching, where the entire water volume oscillates, like the coffee in a cup that you carry to a waiting guest.

In order to create a model of the seiching we look at water in a rectangular container. The length of the container is $L$ and the depth of the water is $h$. Assume that the surface of the water to begin with makes a small angle with the horizontal. The seiching will then start, and we assume that the water surface continues to be plane but oscillates around an axis in the horizontal plane and located in the middle of the container.

Create a model of the movement of the water and derive a formula for the oscillation period $T$. The starting conditions are given in figure above.

Assume that $\xi<<h$. The table below shows experimental oscillation periods for different water depths in two containers of different lengths. Check in some reasonable way how well the formula that you have derived agrees with the experimental data. Give your opinion on the quality of your model.

Table 1. $L=479 \mathrm{~mm}$

| $h / m m$ | 30 | 50 | 69 | 88 | 107 | 124 | 142 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $T / s$ | 1.78 | 1.40 | 1.18 | 1.08 | 1.00 | 0.91 | 0.82 |

Table 2. $L=143 \mathrm{~mm}$

| $h / m m$ | 31 | 38 | 58 | 67 | 124 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | ---: |
| $T / s$ | 0.52 | 0.52 | 0.43 | 0.35 | 0.28 |

The graph below shows results from measurements in lake Vättern in Sweden. This lake has a length of $123 \mathrm{~km}$ and a mean depth of $50 \mathrm{~m}$. What is the time scale in the graph?

<image_2>

The water surface level in Bastudalen (northern end of lake Vättern) and Jönköping (southern end).
field_audit_records.after_value:
In certain lakes there is a strange phenomenon called "seiching" which is an oscillation of the water. Lakes in which you can see this phenomenon are normally long compared with the depth and also narrow. It is natural to see waves in a lake but not something like the seiching, where the entire water volume oscillates, like the coffee in a cup that you carry to a waiting guest.

In order to create a model of the seiching we look at water in a rectangular container. The length of the container is $L$ and the depth of the water is $h$. Assume that the surface of the water to begin with makes a small angle with the horizontal. The seiching will then start, and we assume that the water surface continues to be plane but oscillates around an axis in the horizontal plane and located in the middle of the container.

Create a model of the movement of the water and derive a formula for the oscillation period $T$. The starting conditions are given in the figure above.

Assume that $\xi \ll h$. The table below shows experimental oscillation periods for different water depths in two containers of different lengths. Check in some reasonable way how well the formula that you have derived agrees with the experimental data. Give your opinion on the quality of your model.

Table 1. $L = 479\ \mathrm{mm}$

| $h / \mathrm{mm}$ | 30 | 50 | 69 | 88 | 107 | 124 | 142 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $T / \mathrm{s}$ | 1.78 | 1.40 | 1.18 | 1.08 | 1.00 | 0.91 | 0.82 |

Table 2. $L = 143\ \mathrm{mm}$

| $h / \mathrm{mm}$ | 31 | 38 | 58 | 67 | 124 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $T / \mathrm{s}$ | 0.52 | 0.52 | 0.43 | 0.35 | 0.28 |

The graph below shows results from measurements in lake Vättern in Sweden. This lake has a length of $123\ \mathrm{km}$ and a mean depth of $50\ \mathrm{m}$. What is the time scale in the graph?

The water surface level in Bastudalen (northern end of lake Vättern) and Jönköping (southern end).
